title: Seeing Both Sides
description: Analyze a situation from different people's perspectives.
canDo: Can analyze and present different perspectives on an issue using ~からすると and ~にとって.
sampleDialogue: 学生からすると授業料の値上げは大変ですが、大学にとっては必要なことかもしれません。
practicePrompts: Present how students might feel about a university policy change using ~からすると.
Explain what the same change means from the university's perspective using ~にとって.
Ask your partner which perspective they find more convincing and why.

title: Taking a Stand
description: Commit to one perspective on a contested topic and defend it against counterarguments, using your opponent's viewpoint to strengthen your own case.
canDo: Can defend a chosen perspective and acknowledge the opposing view using ~にとって while maintaining a clear argument.
sampleDialogue: 私にとって、テレワークは生産性を下げるどころか、むしろ集中できる環境を作ってくれます。反対意見もわかりますが、それは職種によって違うと思います。
practicePrompts: State clearly which side of a debate you are taking and explain what the core issue means ~にとって.
Your opponent raises a counterargument from their perspective using ~からすると. Acknowledge it fairly, then explain why your position still holds.
Close your argument by summarizing why your standpoint is more persuasive, drawing on at least one point from the opposing side.

title: Can We Really Do That?
description: Debate whether a proposed plan is realistic or unavoidable.
canDo: Can debate the feasibility of plans using ~わけにはいかない and ~ざるを得ない.
sampleDialogue: 締め切りが明日なので、今夜は徹夜せざるを得ないかもしれません。
practicePrompts: Explain why you cannot skip a certain responsibility using ~わけにはいかない.
Describe a situation where you had no choice but to take action using ~ざるを得ない.
Propose an alternative plan and ask your teammate whether it is feasible.

title: Explaining a Dilemma
titleJa: 板挟みの状況を説明する
description: Describe a real situation in which you were caught between two pressures with no comfortable exit, and walk your listener through why neither option was acceptable.
canDo: Can articulate the constraints of a no-win situation using ~わけにはいかない and ~ざるを得ない in combination.
sampleDialogue: 上司の頼みを断るわけにはいかないし、かといって約束を破らざるを得なかったので、本当に困りました。
practicePrompts: Set up the dilemma: describe two conflicting obligations you faced and why ignoring either one was not an option, using ~わけにはいかない for at least one.
Explain which path you ultimately chose and what forced your hand, using ~ざるを得ない.
Reflect with your colleague on whether there was any third option you did not see at the time, and what you would do differently.

title: Feelings I Cannot Hide
description: Share strong feelings about recent experiences with a close friend.
canDo: Can express strong emotions and uncontrollable feelings using ~てたまらない and ~てしかたがない.
sampleDialogue: 来月の留学が楽しみでたまらないんです。
practicePrompts: Tell your friend about something you are extremely excited about using ~てたまらない.
Describe a worry or frustration you cannot help feeling using ~てしかたがない.
Listen to your friend's feelings and respond with empathy, then share a similar experience.

title: Physical and Emotional Contrasts
description: Compare how two people in the identical situation feel very differently, and explore why the same event can trigger such contrasting reactions.
canDo: Can contrast one person's overwhelming feelings with another's using ~てたまらない and ~てしかたがない to show divergent emotional responses.
sampleDialogue: 私は発表が緊張してたまらないのに、田中さんは全然平気そうで、うらやましくてしかたがないです。
practicePrompts: Describe a shared situation — a performance, a job interview, a trip — and explain how you feel about it using ~てたまらない or ~てしかたがない.
Your partner describes feeling the opposite way. Express mild disbelief or curiosity, and ask them why they feel so differently.
Together, explore what personal background or personality trait might explain the contrasting reactions.

title: Who Should Take Responsibility?
description: Discuss responsibilities and obligations in a workplace scenario.
canDo: Can discuss obligations and nuanced responsibility using ~べき and ~わけではない.
sampleDialogue: ミスをした人が謝るべきだと思いますが、全部その人のせいというわけではありません。
practicePrompts: State what you think should be done about a workplace mistake using ~べき.
Add nuance by explaining that something is not necessarily the case using ~わけではない.
Ask your colleague how responsibility should be shared in a team setting.

title: Distributing Responsibility
titleJa: 責任の分担を話し合う
description: Work through a messy real-world situation to decide who bears how much responsibility, and push back when blame is assigned too simply.
canDo: Can distribute and qualify degrees of responsibility among multiple parties using ~べき and ~わけではない.
sampleDialogue: プロジェクトが失敗したのはリーダーだけが悪いというわけではなくて、チーム全体で改善策を考えるべきだと思います。
practicePrompts: Present a situation in which blame has been placed unfairly on one person. Argue that responsibility is shared, using ~わけではない to soften the accusation.
Assign specific obligations to each party involved — who should apologize, who should compensate, who should change their process — using ~べき.
Your colleague disagrees with your breakdown. Negotiate a final shared conclusion about how responsibility should be handled going forward.

title: Worth the Effort?
description: Evaluate whether a challenging experience was worthwhile.
canDo: Can evaluate experiences and make qualified judgments using ~に値する and ~といっても.
sampleDialogue: あの経験は大変でしたが、挑戦するに値するものでした。
practicePrompts: Describe a difficult experience and explain why it was worthwhile using ~に値する.
Qualify a statement by explaining it is not as extreme as it sounds using ~といっても.
Ask your mentor about a challenge they faced and whether they would recommend it.

title: Revisiting a Failed Attempt
description: Look back on an attempt that did not succeed and honestly evaluate whether the effort was still worthwhile, even with the outcome.
canDo: Can evaluate a failed attempt fairly, acknowledging both its limitations and its value using ~といっても and ~に値する.
sampleDialogue: 結果は出なかったといっても、あの挑戦は十分試みるに値したと今でも思っています。
practicePrompts: Describe an attempt that did not go as planned. Use ~といっても to prevent your friend from overstating how badly it went.
Argue that the attempt was still worth making using ~に値する, pointing to what you gained from the process itself.
Your friend asks whether you would try again under different conditions. Discuss what would have to change for the effort to clearly be worth repeating.

title: Building a Case
description: Construct a well-rounded argument on a topic by combining multiple grammar points.
canDo: Can synthesize a structured argument combining multiple B2 grammar points.
sampleDialogue: この問題は簡単に解決できるわけではありませんが、議論するに値するテーマだと思います。
practicePrompts: State your position on a topic and explain why it matters, using at least two B2 grammar points.
Acknowledge the opposing view using ~からすると, then counter with ~ざるを得ない or ~べき.
Summarize your argument in two sentences, combining grammar from lessons 7 through 11.

title: Structured Mini-Debate
description: Engage in a structured two-sided debate on a real social topic, with each side making at least six turns and drawing on the full range of B2 grammar from lessons 7 through 11.
canDo: Can sustain a structured multi-turn debate on a complex topic, deploying all B2 grammar points from Quartet 2 lessons 7–11 in context.
sampleDialogue: AIにとって感情を持つことは不可能というわけではありませんが、人間からすると、やはり信頼するに値するかどうか慎重に考えざるを得ません。
practicePrompts: Opening (3 turns each): State your position and its rationale. Use ~からすると or ~にとって to frame the issue, and ~べき to assert your main claim. The opposing side responds with ~わけではない to qualify your claim.

Development (2 turns each): Introduce the strongest objection to your own side using ~といっても, then rebut it. Use ~ざるを得ない or ~わけにはいかない to explain why your position still holds. The opposing side presses back with ~てたまらない or ~てしかたがない to convey the urgency of their concern.

Closing (1 turn each): Deliver a closing statement that uses ~に値する to affirm why the debate itself matters, and synthesize at least three grammar points from the lesson set into two to three sentences.
